coredata.dc:title: Red light-emitting carbon dots for reduced phototoxicity and photothermal/photodynamic-enhanced synergistic tumor therapy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Luming
Cheng, Hui
Ren, Zhishuang
Wang, Huili
Lu, Junya
Zhang, Jinghai
Zhao, Qinfu
Wang, Siling
coredata.dc:description: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has received extensive attention due to its non-invasive, photo-stimuli-responsive advantages. However, the poor dispersibility of most photosensitizers in water and non-negligible phototoxicity limit their further clinical application. To surmount this dilemma, red-emitting carbon dots (RCDs) with good photothermal conversion ability were synthesized, and photosensitizers Chlorin-e6 (Ce6) were linked onto RCDs to construct the nano-platform RCDs-Ce6 through amide reaction. In this system, RCDs can increase the dispersion of Ce6 to enhance PDT and absorb partitions of daylight to reduce phototoxicity. At the same time, RCDs-Ce6 can achieve simultaneous excitation of photothermal therapy (PTT) and PDT under a single laser, which can overcome the drawbacks of monotherapy while simplifying the treatment procedure. Besides, the excellent fluorescence imaging capability of RCDs-Ce6 could achieve imaging-guided tumor treatment. Spontaneously, both the cytological and 3D multicellular spheroids (3D MCS) levels demonstrated that RCDs-Ce6 had a stronger synergic therapeutic effect with a combination index (CI) of 0.278 and lower phototoxicity. Meanwhile, in vivo experiments showed that RCDs-Ce6 (PTT and PDT) had a more substantial tumor inhibition effect and fluorescence imaging ability with more tumor distribution. This study widened the way for further applications of photodynamic-enhanced therapy with reduced phototoxicity in photosensitizer delivery based on RCDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red-emitting carbon dots
Photodynamic-enhanced therapy
Reduced phototoxicity
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Synergistic tumor therapy

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and prescription screening of RCDs
Photothermal properties of RCDs and RCDs-Ce6
Photothermal effects of RCDs-Ce6 and combination therapy in vivo
Fluorescence stability and cellular photothermal properties
Cellular photodynamic properties and mechanism of apoptosis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Youth Project of Liaoning Provincial Education Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Grants from China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021M690483 ), Youth Project of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (No. LJKQZ2021030 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) AFM 3D image of RCDs. (B) AFM topography image of RCDs. (C) Height profile of RCDs. (D) TEM image of RCDs. (E) Particle size and distribution of RCDs. (F) Sodium fluorescein and RCDs emission spectra.

(A) 1H NMR spectra of RCDs, Ce6 and RCDs-Ce6. (B) XPS survey spectra of RCDs and RCDs-Ce6. (C) FT-IR spectra of RCDs, Ce6 and RCDs-Ce6. (D) Zeta potential of RCDs, Ce6 and RCDs-Ce6. (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of RCDs, Ce6 and RCDs-Ce6. Photothermal stability of (F) RCDs and (G) RCDs-Ce6. Linear time data versus -Lnθof (H) RCDs and (I) RCDs-Ce6 (curves inserted were temperature variations for vehicles.).

Excitation and emission spectra of (A) RCDs and (D) RCDs-Ce6. Photoluminescent emission spectra of (B) RCDs and (E) RCDs-Ce6 at different excitation wavelengths. (C) The fluorescence quenching ability of RCDs, RCDs-Ce6, and free Ce6 with 655 nm laser (500 mW/cm2) (n = 3). (F) The fluorescence intensity changes at the characteristic peak of SOSG in different groups (n = 3). (G) Schematic illustration of ROS generation of RCDs-Ce6 exposed to daylight and 655 nm laser. Absorbance of ABDA, RCDs-Ce6, Ce6, Ce6/ABDA and RCDs-Ce6/ABDA solution under different irradiation times of (H) daylight (White fluorescent lamp with a power of 30 W (light intensity = 800 W/m2) was used to simulate daylight conditions with a predominant wavelength range of 300–750 nm) and (I) laser (n = 3).

(A) Fluorescence microscope images of 4T1 cells cultivated with different conditions. (B) Fluorescence microscope images of 4T1 cells separately incubated with RCDs-Ce6 and free Ce6 with laser for different times, respectively. The intracellular ROS generation with laser by CLSM (C) and FCM (D). (E) The fluorescence intensity of Rh123 when the 4T1 cells were incubated with free Ce6, RCDs, and RCDs-Ce6 with laser (500 mW/cm2) by FCM. (F) The cellular photothermal effect of 4T1 cells was incubated with different concentrations of RCDs-Ce6 with 655 nm laser (n = 3). (Scale bars = 100 µm).

Cell viability of 4T1 cell in RCDs, RCDs-Ce6, Ce6 under (A) daylight and (B) 655 nm laser (500 mW/cm2). (C) Microscopy photographs of 3D MCS undergoing different treatments (The treatment group was given 655 nm laser irradiation at 500 mW/cm2) (Scale bars = 200 µm). (D) Calcein-AM/PI co-staining fluorescence images of 4T1 cells incubated with PBS (blank), free Ce6 (PDT), RCDs (PTT), and Ce6-RCDs (PDT and PTT) with 655 nm laser (500 mW/cm2) by CLSM (Scale bars = 100 µm). (E) FCM analysis of apoptosis based on Annexin V-FITC apoptosis kit for 4T1 cells followed different treatments. (* p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001).

(A) In vivo fluorescence images at different time points after i.v. injection of RCDs-Ce6 and Ce6. (B) Fluorescence images and (C) Mean fluorescence intensities of main organs and tumors after intravenous injection for 24 h (n = 3). (* p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001).

(A) Thermal imaging photographs of mice treated with saline and RCDs-Ce6 under 655 nm laser (500 mW/cm2). (B) The heating curves of the tumors after administrated with RCDs-Ce6 for 0, 2, 8, and 24 h. (C) Body weight evolution curves (n = 5). (D) Profiles of relative tumor volume (n = 5). (E) Relative tumor mass of excised tumors (n = 5). (F) The photograph of the tumor was excised on the 14th day from different groups. (G) H&E staining photographs of major organs harvested from mice of different groups (Scale bars = 50 µm). (* p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001).

(A) Preparation procedures of RCDs-Ce6 drug delivery nanoplatform. (B) Schematic illustration of the fluorescence imaging capability of RCDs-Ce6 and the realization of PTT/PDT-enhanced synergistic tumor therapy under 655 nm laser irradiation.

head.ce:title: Red light-emitting carbon dots for reduced phototoxicity and photothermal/photodynamic-enhanced synergistic tumor therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenyang, Liaoning Province 110016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutics, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenyang, Liaoning Province 110016, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering, School of Medical Devices, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang 110016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biomedical Engineering, School of Medical Devices, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biomedical Engineering, School of Medical Devices, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang 110016, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has received extensive attention due to its non-invasive, photo-stimuli-responsive advantages. However, the poor dispersibility of most photosensitizers in water and non-negligible phototoxicity limit their further clinical application. To surmount this dilemma, red-emitting carbon dots (RCDs) with good photothermal conversion ability were synthesized, and photosensitizers Chlorin-e6 (Ce6) were linked onto RCDs to construct the nano-platform RCDs-Ce6 through amide reaction. In this system, RCDs can increase the dispersion of Ce6 to enhance PDT and absorb partitions of daylight to reduce phototoxicity. At the same time, RCDs-Ce6 can achieve simultaneous excitation of photothermal therapy (PTT) and PDT under a single laser, which can overcome the drawbacks of monotherapy while simplifying the treatment procedure. Besides, the excellent fluorescence imaging capability of RCDs-Ce6 could achieve imaging-guided tumor treatment. Spontaneously, both the cytological and 3D multicellular spheroids (3D MCS) levels demonstrated that RCDs-Ce6 had a stronger synergic therapeutic effect with a combination index (CI) of 0.278 and lower phototoxicity. Meanwhile, in vivo experiments showed that RCDs-Ce6 (PTT and PDT) had a more substantial tumor inhibition effect and fluorescence imaging ability with more tumor distribution. This study widened the way for further applications of photodynamic-enhanced therapy with reduced phototoxicity in photosensitizer delivery based on RCDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a traditional tumor treatment method, chemotherapy is one of the most general therapeutic methods in the clinic application. However, chemotherapy drugs often kill healthy tissue owing to poor targeting [1]. Therefore, precise and controllable cancer treatment methods are the direction that researchers are moving forward to. Over the past few decades, the integrated tumor diagnosis and treatment system has become an emerging research field. Imaging agents and drug molecules were co-loaded in nanoparticles to realize the organic combination of tumor therapy and diagnosis [2]. The integrated nano-platform of diagnosis and treatment can improve the anti-tumor efficacy of drugs and realize precise treatment guided by imaging [3]. At the same time, the light-triggered theranostics has been recognized as an attractive therapy treatment due to their noninvasive and photo-stimuli-responsive features, such as PTT and PDT [4–6].

PTT, killing tumor cells by converting light energy to heat through photo-absorbing molecules or nanoparticles, is considered one of the most promising technologies for treating tumors due to its high accuracy and low side effects [7]. Due to low biological tissue absorption, the laser of red to the near-infrared region (650–950 nm) is often used as the excitation source for PTT. The ideal PTT agent should have strong photoabsorption, low toxicity, and wealthy functional groups to achieve tumor-targeted therapy [8].

In recent years, diagnostic and therapeutic preparations with photothermal conversion functions have been developed mainly based on inorganic nanomaterials such as gold and copper sulfide [9–11]. Although these inorganic nanomaterials usually have significant photothermal effects, their non-biodegradable properties will lead to long-term retention in vivo, resulting in long-term toxicity. Therefore, researchers were focusing on new nano-phototherapy agents with better biocompatibility to accelerate the clinical applications of PTT technology [12].

Owing to its excellent bioimaging, photothermal and abundant surface functional groups, CDs, a newly emerged type of carbon nanomaterials, are attracting more attention as a next-generation nano-platform for integrating diagnosis and treatment [13]. However, blue-emitting CDs fail to avoid the interference of the background fluorescence and optical absorption of biological tissue, which significantly hinders the application of CDs as a tracer. Generally, the longer emission wavelength of light tends to cause weak optical absorption of biological tissues, resulting in a lower background fluorescence and more substantial light penetration into tissues. Thus, red-emitting CDs may offer excellent tracer and location potential in integrating diagnosis and treatment.

PDT is a technique for treating tumor disease that combines photosensitizing medications with light activation. Applying a specific wavelength of light to irradiate the lesion site so that the photosensitizers in tumor tissues undergo a violent photochemical reaction and selectively destroy tumor cells [14,15]. It is a minimally invasive or noninvasive technique. Compared with traditional drugs, PDT has the advantages of an immediate curative effect, minor side effects, and a simple method [10,16].

The past decade has witnessed an emerging interest in the use of Chlorin-e6 (Ce6) in the realm of PDT. The growing interest in Ce6 as specifical photosensitizers has been due to their appealing characteristics, including strong absorption in the red light region and solid photodynamic response. These qualities are superior to porphyrin, Verteporfin, and other traditional photosensitizers. Unfortunately, Ce6 shows limited applications in tumor therapy due to poor tumor targeting, aggregation tendency in water, and nonnegligible phototoxicity due to strong absorption at 405 nm [17,18].

However, the insufficient treatment effect of PTT and limited diffusion distance of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in PDT hinder their further clinical application [19]. Synergistic therapy tends to be more effective than monotherapy. Researchers have made great efforts in the synergistic treatment of PTT + PDT and have achieved many dazzling achievements [20–22]. For example, in our previous work, Ce6 was loaded into multifunctional mesoporous carbon-manganese nanocomposite to realize the combined therapy of PTT + PDT and showed an excellent tumor suppressor effect [23]. However, in these studies [23–25], PTT and PDT were often stimulated separately by lasers of different wavelengths due to the distant absorption peaks of photothermal agents and photosensitizers, resulting in longer treatment time and poor patient tolerance. Therefore, constructing a system that could stimulate PTT and PDT simultaneously with a single laser is a meaningful attempt [26].

To verify the application of CDs in drug delivery and enhance the efficacy of photosensitizers in tumor therapy, amino-rich, RCDs with good photothermal conversion ability were synthesized using p-phenylenediamine (PPD). Furthermore, a small amount of photosensitizer Ce6 was grafted to RCDs through an amide reaction, which enabled the system to maintain the PTT ability and fluorescence emission of RCDs while obtaining photodynamic activity. RCDs increase the dispersion of Ce6 and absorb some daylight, which may enhance the PDT effect and reduce the phototoxicity of the system. In parallel, the excellent absorption of RCDs and Ce6 in the red-light range can be excited simultaneously with the 655 nm laser, simplifying treatment procedures and increasing patient compliance. Intriguingly, owing to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between RCDs and Ce6, the fluorescence emission wavelength was redshifted, and the photodynamic activity may increase [27], which was beneficial to improving the therapeutic effect and fluorescence imaging ability. Herein, RCDs-Ce6 were expected to have excellent fluorescence imaging ability and PDT enhancement while simultaneously exciting PTT and PDT under the single laser. Scheme. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and prescription screening of RCDs
ce:section.ce:para: PPD was used as precursors, and a series of CDs with different properties were prepared in different solvents (dichloromethane, ethyl acetate, demethylketene, acetonitrile, dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), methanol, and dimethyl formamide), respectively. The solution was transferred to Teflon stainless steel reactor, placed in a Muffle furnace for 5 h at 200 ℃. The crude products were separated and purified to obtain pure samples with a silica column. The silica gel column was filled with a 1:1 mixture of ethyl acetate and methanol as eluent. Crude CDs were added and fluorescent layers were collected. The purified CDs were obtained after drying and removing the eluent. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs in each sample was calculated by reference method (Formula 1).

10 mg Ce6 and 2 mg DMT-MM (4-(4,6-Dimethoxy-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)− 4-methyl morpholinium chloride) were added into a 10 mL brown vial. 50 mg RCDs were dispersed in 2 mL DMSO and then added into the above vial, stirring for 24 h. 1000D ultrafiltration tubes were used to remove unreacted Ce6 from the reaction solution, repeated for three times. Purified RCDs-Ce6 was obtained after drying, and the loading rate of Ce6 was calculated (Formula 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal properties of RCDs and RCDs-Ce6
ce:section.ce:para: RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 dispersion (200 μg/mL, 200 μL) was irradiated by 655 nm laser (500 mW/cm2) for 200 s to calculate the photothermal conversion efficiency. The stability of the photothermal conversion properties of RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 was verified by irradiating 655 nm laser. At the same time, the effects of concentration and power on the heating effect were studied.

Incandescent lamps were used to simulate daylight in the clean bench (HCB-1300 V, Haier, China). 9, 10-Anthracenediyl-bis(methylene)-dimalonic acid (ABDA) was prepared as ROS probe, and Ce6 solution, RCDs-Ce6 suspension, ABDA solution, Ce6 + ABDA solution and RCDs-Ce6 + ABDA suspension were prepared respectively (at the corresponding Ce6 concentration of 1 mg/mL). The above solutions were irradiated with 655 nm laser (500 mW/cm2) and daylight, and the ROS effect was determined.

Singlet oxygen sensor green reagent (SOSG) (2.5 μM) as ROS probe, RCDs-Ce6 + SOSG, Ce6 + SOSG, RCDs + SOSG (at the corresponding Ce6 concentration of 6 μg/mL) and SOSG was prepared respectively. The above solutions were irradiated with 655 nm laser (500 mW/cm2) at different time, and RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to measure the fluorescence intensity.

A fluorescence microscope was used to analyze the cellular uptake behavior of nanoparticles. In a nutshell, 4T1 cells were seeded into 24-well plates and incubated with RCDs, Ce6, and RCDs-Ce6 (at the corresponding Ce6 concentration of 1.5 μg/mL) with different conditions for 2 h, followed by staining the cell nuclei with Hoechst 33258. Cellular uptake was observed by fluorescence microscopy and CLSM. Additionally, fixed cells that have taken up the drug were irradiated with laser (655 nm, 500 mW/cm2) for different periods, and the fluorescence quenching was observed with a fluorescence microscope.

For in vitro biological investigations, the 4T1 cell was utilized, which was incubated in RPMI-1640 medium with FBS. After being incubated for 24 h, cells were treated with different concentrations of RCDs-Ce6 (0, 15, 25, 100 μg/mL) and then incubated for 2 h. After cell digestion, collection, and resuspension, samples were routinely exposed to a 500 mW/cm2 655 nm laser for 3 min. The temperature changes were recorded with an IR camera.

2′,7′-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein Diacetate (DCFH-DA) was used to detect cell ROS. 4T1 cells were seeded in 6-well plates and cultured for 24 h. Free Ce6 and RCDs-Ce6 (at the corresponding Ce6 concentration of 1.5 μg/mL) were added and incubated with cells for 2 h. After that, 25 μM DCFH-DA was added and incubated for 15 min. Cells were collected after 3 min of irradiation with 655 nm laser. The ROS content of each group was detected by flow cytometry (FCM) and fluorescence spectrophotometer. CLSM was used to observe intracellular ROS production after laser irradiation.

4T1 cells were cultivated in 96-well plates (1 × 104 cells per well) for the cytotoxic assay. Subdivided each group into two subgroups: laser group and daylight group with different concentrations (at the corresponding Ce6 concentration of 0.01–10 μg/mL). The former was irradiated with 655 nm laser for 5 min (500 mW/cm2). The latter was exposed to daylight for 30 min. Finally, cell viability was determined by MTT assay. Calculation of the combination index (CI) by Formula 3. 4T1 cells were seeded into 24-well plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well. The cells were incubated with RCDs, free Ce6, and RCDs-Ce6 (at the corresponding Ce6 concentration of 1.5 μg/mL) for 2 h, respectively. In the daylight and laser groups, cells were irradiated for 30 min and 5 min respectively. Following staining with Calcein-AM/PI, the cells were placed in CLSM for observation.

4T1 cells were seeded in 6-well plates and cultured for 24 h. Free Ce6, RCDs, and RCDs-Ce6 (at the corresponding Ce6 concentration of 1.5 μg/mL) were added and incubated with cells for 2 h. 4T1 cells were harvested after 5 min of 655 nm laser (500 mW/cm2) irradiation. The Rh 123 and Annexin V-FITC apoptosis kit was used to detect apoptosis by FCM.

4T1 cells were cultivated in 96-well plates (1 ×104 cells per well) for 24 h and treated with RCDs-Ce6 (at the corresponding Ce6 concentration of 1.0 μg/mL). There were two special treatment groups: one group pretreated a dose of 1 mM Vitamin C (Vc) before receiving irradiation with 655 nm laser (500 mW/cm2, 5 min) to study the PTT effect of RCDs-Ce6 alone, and the other group was irradiated with 655 nm laser (500 mW/cm2, 5 min) in 37 ℃ water bath to study the PDT effect of RCDs-Ce6 alone. Meanwhile, the control group and the RCDs-Ce6 group without special treatment were studied simultaneously. Then MTT assay was used to assess cell viability.

NIH-3T3 cells were used to study the toxicity of drugs to normal cell lines. NIH-3T3 cells were cultivated in 96-well plates (1 × 104 cells per well) and cultured for 24 h. RCDs, free Ce6, and RCDs-Ce6 (at the corresponding Ce6 concentration of 1.0 μg/mL) were added and incubated with cells for 2 h, respectively. Each group was irradiated with 655 nm laser (500 mW/cm2) for 5 min, and MTT assay was used to study the cell viability.

96-well agarose plates containing 4T1 cells were seeded at a density of 6 × 103 cells per well. Different treatment regimens were given after 3D MCS reached about 300–400 µm (at the corresponding Ce6 concentration of 1.0 μg/mL). Laser group was exposed to 655 nm laser (500 mW/cm2) for 10 min (0, 1, 3, 5 day). The daylight group was exposed for 30 min (0, 1, 3, 5 day). The diameter changes of 3D MCS were observed by an optical microscope.

Shenyang Pharmaceutical University provided 18–20 g of female Balb/c mice. ARRIVE guidelines of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University's Animal Protection and Ethics Committee were followed during the experiments. Biodistribution experiments were then carried out on 4T1 tumor-bearing mice: In the right rear of the mice, 1 × 107 4T1 cells were administered subcutaneously. The animals were divided randomly into two groups when the tumors were measured to reach 200 mm3 (volume = width2 × length/2). The IVIS imaging system (excitation wavelength of 510 nm with a 700 nm emission filter) was used to take fluorescent images after intravenous injection of RCDs, Ce6 and RCDs-Ce6 (at the corresponding Ce6 concentration of 1.0 mg/kg) at different time. 24 h after, mice were sacrificed, and their major organs and tumors were excised for fluorescence intensity measurements. Calculating the mean fluorescence intensity of each group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal effects of RCDs-Ce6 and combination therapy in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Tumor mouse model was established as in 2.10. The photothermal effect was measured in tumor-bearing Balb/c mice. When the tumor volume reached 200 cm3, the saline and RCDs-Ce6 (Ce6 does of 1.0 mg/kg) were intravenously injected into the mice. Using 655 nm laser to irradiate the tumor site at various time points (0 h (control group), 2 h, 8 h, 24 h) after administration, and the temperature changes were recorded by an IR camera.

The tumor-bearing mice were divided into five groups and treated with normal saline (control), free Ce6 + laser, RCDs + laser, RCDs-Ce6, and RCDs-Ce6 (at the corresponding Ce6 concentration of 1.0 mg/kg) + laser, respectively. In treatment groups, the tumor regions were exposed to 655 nm laser (500 mW/cm2) for 6 min after administration for 2 h. All groups were treated on the 0, 3, and 6th days. The mice's body weights and tumor volumes were measured during the treatment period. Biochemical indicators after treatment of saline and RCDs-Ce6 were studied. Finally, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) were used to stain the main organs and tumors.

PPD was used as precursors, and a series of CDs with different properties were prepared in different solvents. The purified CDs were dispersed and placed under UV lamp to observe and measure the emission peak and QY (Fig. S1, Table S1). RCDs were characterized by various methods to measure their basic physical properties. According to the AFM 3D diagram of RCDs and the corresponding height distribution diagram ( Fig. 1A-C), it can be seen that the size of RCDs was relatively uniform with good dispersion in pH 7.4 PBS and the average size of RCDs was about 4.3 nm. As can be seen from Fig. 1D, RCDs showed a uniform spheroidal structure with good dispersion. HRTEM showed that RCDs had apparent lattice spacing (Fig. S7), which was the same as the crystal plane spacing of graphite sp2 hybrid carbon, indicating that RCDs had a graphite-like structure. As shown in Fig. 1E, the particle size of RCDs was mainly distributed in the range of 2.5–5.5 nm, calculated by statistical analysis with an average particle size of 4.2 nm. As shown in Fig. S2, RCDs had a large and broad diffraction peak at 21.5° by XRD analysis, indicating the amorphous structure of RCDs. CDs synthesized in DMF were screened out with good comprehensive performance, namely RCDs. Calculated by the reference method, the QY of RCDs was 17.76% (Fig. 1F). The results showed that RCDs were in a graphite-like amorphous structure and had a homogeneous spheroid structure with a QY of 17.76%. In summary, RCDs had good dispersion and super-small properties that may allow for more efficient drug delivery as well as greater permeability in tumor therapy [28].

Due to the distinct proton peak (-NH2) at 4.25 ppm and the wide absorption band between 3200 and 3400 cm-1 with the positive Zeta potential (RCDs group in Fig. 2A, C, D). These results indicated that RCDs owned rich -NH2 functional groups on their surfaces, which facilitated their surface functionalization and subsequent modifications, such as amidation and esterification reactions. Therefore, Ce6 was expected to graft on the surface of RCDs through amide reaction with DMT-MM as a carboxyl activator [29] to obtain functionalized RCDs-Ce6. The synthesis of RCDs-Ce6 was characterized using various methods. As shown in Fig. 2A, the proton peak (-NH2) intensity of RCDs-Ce6 at 4.25 ppm decreased significantly. And the characteristic proton peaks of the amide structure were observed at 9.6–9.8 ppm, indicating that Ce6 was covalently attached to the surface of RCDs. As shown in Fig. 2B, the XPS spectrum of RCDs-Ce6 shows three peaks at 284.8, 399.9, and 531.2 eV corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. After integral calculation, the C: N: O ratio was 60.2: 28.8: 11.0, in which the content of C and O was higher than that of RCDs (C: N: O = 59.71:31.37:8.92), indicating that Ce6 was grafted onto the surface of RCDs. Compared with RCDs, the absorption peak intensity of -NH2 in RCDs-Ce6 at 3200 cm-1 decreased (Fig. 2C). At the same time, the absorption peak intensity of the amide bond at 1630 cm-1 and 3373.7 cm-1 increased. The changes in the intensity of these absorption peaks indicated that Ce6 was successfully connected to the surface of RCDs. The Zeta potential of RCDs was + 16.4 mV, as shown in Fig. 2D, caused by the positive -NH2 groups on the surface of RCDs. After the Ce6 was grafted on RCDs, the Zeta potential of RCDs-Ce6 was decreased to + 10.0 mV. This change was because the negatively charged -COOH of Ce6 reacted with the -NH2 of RCDs, triggering the declined amount of -NH2 groups on RCDs-Ce6. As shown in Fig. 2E, Ce6 exhibited strong Soret absorption at 405 nm, and Q-bands were between 500 and 700 nm. Both RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 showed wide absorption bands extending to about 750 nm. This extension of UV–vis absorption to the near-infrared region was beneficial to the potential function of PTT. In addition, compared with RCDs, RCDs-Ce6 also showed an absorption peak at 405 nm, which was attributed to the Soret absorption of Ce6. These results indicated that RCDs were successfully grafted with Ce6. The TEM image of RCDs-Ce6 revealed a homogeneous graphitic structure with an average size distribution of about 4.5 nm. At the same time, the uniform size distribution of RCDs-Ce6 was about 9.08 nm by dynamic light scattering (Fig. S10), which was increased compared with TEM image (4.5 nm). This may be related to the forming of a thick hydrated layer in water around RCDs-Ce6. The loading rate of Ce6 was calculated to be 5.95% in RCDs-Ce6 by Formula 2.

The photothermal conversion capacity is a vital indicator in evaluating the PTT effect. As shown in Fig. S3, S4, the temperature of the RCD and RCDs-Ce6 increased expeditiously under continuous irradiation, while the temperature of pH 7.4 PBS was almost constant under the same irradiation. The photothermal effect of RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 showed concentration-dependent, irradiation time-dependent, and power density-dependent patterns. For the photothermal stability of RCDs and RCDs-Ce6, Fig. 2F, G displayed that almost no observable attenuation occurred during the irradiation and natural cooling for 4 repeats. The photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 was 23.27% and 16.28%, shown in Fig. 2H, 2I, and inserted illustration. The photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of RCDs-Ce6 was slightly lower than that of RCDs. This may be due to the fact that the grafting of Ce6 with RCDs increased the absorbance (Aλ in Formula 4) of RCDs-Ce6 at 655 nm (Fig. 2E) while the Ce6 part may not participate in the warming and thus the overall photothermal conversion efficiency of RCDs-Ce6 decreased. To sum up, RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 had constant photothermal conversion ability and good photo-stability. This provided the basic conditions for the stable PTT effect of RCDs and RCDs-Ce6.

Fluorescent properties of RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 were carried out to explore the changes in optical properties before and after the grafting of Ce6. As can be seen from Fig. 3A-B, the excitation and emission peaks of RCDs were located at 500 nm and 625 nm, showing the apparent red fluorescence signals. And the emission peak of RCDs did not change with the increase of excitation wavelength, indicating the excitation-independent fluorescent property of RCDs. Compared with RCDs, the emission peak of RCDs-Ce6 was slightly redshifted to 634 nm (Fig. 3D-E). Calculated by the reference method, the QY of RCDs-Ce6 was 21.82% (Fig. S6). Compared with RCDs, the QY of RCDs-Ce6 was slightly improved.

This change was due to the FRET that occurred between RCDs and Ce6. Under the continuous irradiation of 655 nm laser (500 mW/cm2), the fluorescence intensity of the Ce6 group decreased by 76.15%. However, the fluorescence intensity of the RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 group decreased by 19.38% and 17.38% within 12 min, respectively (Fig. 3C). Compared with RCDs and RCDs-Ce6, Ce6 was photobleached more quickly. When Ce6 was grafted with RCDs, the photobleaching of RCDs-Ce6 was weakened. This may be due to the more stable fluorescence structure of RCDs as fluorescence emission host in RCDs-Ce6. The low photobleaching of RCDs-Ce6 could ensure the fluorescence intensity of RCDs-Ce6 after repeated laser irradiation, which was significant for the in vivo tracer and imaging ability.

The ability to efficiently generate ROS is crucial for PDT. SOSG was used as a detection probe for ROS. The intensity of the SOSG and RCDs group was almost unchanged, which indicated that pure RCDs could not generate ROS. In the Ce6 and RCDs-Ce6 groups, the fluorescence intensity of SOSG increased with the irradiation time (Fig. 3F). Meanwhile, the intensity of SOSG in the RCDs-Ce6 group was higher than that in the free Ce6 group, which suggested that the RCDs could enhance the ROS production of Ce6. The increase of ROS may be due to the increased dispersion of Ce6 by RCDs [19]. Fig. S11 showed the increase in fluorescence intensity at the particular peak of SOSG (530 nm) as a function of laser irradiation time. This indicated that the amount of ROS produced by RCDs-Ce6 depended on the laser irradiation time. ABDA probe was used to explore ROS generation capacity of Ce6 and RCDs-Ce6 with daylight and 655 nm laser. Under exposure to daylight (Fig. 3H), Ce6 produced small amounts of ROS, while RCDs-Ce6 hardly produced ROS, which indicated that RCDs-Ce6 had negligible phototoxicity. However, under laser irradiation, RCDs-Ce6 exhibited stronger ROS generation capacity compared to free Ce6 (Fig. 3I). The lower phototoxicity of RCDs-Ce6 might be that RCDs absorb most of the daylight, and the absorption peak at 405 nm of RCDs-Ce6 was weaker than that of Ce6 (Fig. 2E), reducing Ce6 absorption of daylight. The stronger ability of ROS generation might be that the grafting of RCDs under 655 nm laser irradiation not only imparted better dispersity to the system but also enhanced the ROS generation capacity of RCDs-Ce6 due to FRET [27]. The electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR) was used to detect ROS and the results showed the excellent singlet oxygen generation ability of RCDs-Ce6 (Fig. S12). In conclusion, RCDs-Ce6 showed stronger ROS generation ability and lower phototoxicity (Fig. 3G). Therefore, the RCDs-Ce6 could be used for excellent in vivo tumor PDT treatment.

Efficient drug uptake is critical to drug efficacy. Compared with free Ce6, the RCDs-Ce6 group had more red fluorescence in the cell interior ( Fig. 4 A), suggesting that the grafting of RCDs could enhance drug uptake by cells. This was because free Ce6 was negatively charged and hard to enter. In contrast, RCDs-Ce6 was positively charged overall and more accessible to be uptaken by cells, which was essential to improve the lethality. At the same time, the intensity of red fluorescence in the RCDs + Laser group was higher than in RCDs group, suggesting that the photothermal effect of RCDs could increase the uptake of drugs by cells. At the same time, apparent red fluorescence of RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 inside 4T1 cells can be observed by CLSM (Fig. S13). The above results verified the excellent fluorescence imaging capability of RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 at the cellular level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence stability and cellular photothermal properties
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the fluorescence stability of RCDs-Ce6 in vitro, 4T1 cells were separately incubated with RCDs-Ce6 and free Ce6 with continuous laser irradiation. As displayed in Fig. 4B, the red fluorescence from RCDs-Ce6 remains intense even after continuous irradiation for 12 min under the excitation. Still, the fluorescence intensity of Ce6 rapidly faded to an almost invisible level after 12 min irradiation. Fig. S14 is the semi-quantification of the fluorescence intensity in Fig. 4B. The fluorescence stability of RCDs-Ce6 was more potent than that of free Ce6. The above results were consistent with Fig. 3C, indicating that RCDs-Ce6 had a more stable fluorescence imaging ability than free Ce6.

The cellular photothermal experiment was conducted further to evaluate the PTT effect of RCDs-Ce6 in 4T1 cells. The results of cellular photothermal experiment are shown in Fig. 4 F. After 4T1 cells were cultured with culture medium containing 15 μg/mL RCDs-Ce6 for 2 h, the temperature of cell suspension increased from 37 oC to 45 oC with 655 nm laser. This temperature is lethal to tumor cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular photodynamic properties and mechanism of apoptosis
ce:section.ce:para: DCFH-DA as a ROS probe reacts with intracellular ROS to generate DCF with green fluorescence. CLSM was used to perform the ability of RCDs-Ce6 to produce ROS in cells, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 C. Cells cultured with RCDs-Ce6 + DCFH-DA could be observed green fluorescence after exposure to 655 nm laser for 10 min. FCM and fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to detect the intracellular ROS content as shown in Fig. 4D and S15–16. Under the same conditions of Ce6 content and irradiation time, the fluorescence intensity of the RCDs-Ce6 group was more vigorous. These results suggested that RCDs-Ce6 had a stronger ROS generation ability, which was consistent with the results in vitro (Fig. 3F-H). The enhanced PDT effect may be due to the increased dispersion of Ce6 provided by RCDs and the FRET effect between Ce6 and RCDs.

Ce6-enriched sites in cells are critical for the induction of apoptosis. Cell necrosis was induced after light treatment when Ce6 is mainly located on the cytoplasmic membrane. However, when Ce6 is gathered in other cellular structures, such as mitochondria, it causes apoptosis after light treatment [30]. Disruption of the mitochondrial membrane is generally considered the first step in apoptosis. Rh123 is widely used as a fluorescent probe to detect mitochondrial membrane potential. When the mitochondrial membrane potential decreases, the fluorescence intensity of Rh123 will weaken. The detection results of Rh123 are shown in Fig. 4E. The Rh123 fluorescence intensity decreased more obviously in the combination treatment group, suggesting that RCDs-Ce6 (PTT and PDT) had a stronger apoptotic effect and that the apoptosis in this treatment might be caused by mitochondrial damage. This result also implied that RCDs-Ce6 were more easily taken up by cells and entered the internal structure than free Ce6.

MTT assay was used to evaluate the cell relative survival rates, as shown in Fig. 5 A-B. The survival rates of cells incubated with RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 under daylight were more than 80%, indicating that RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 had negligible toxicity and exemplary safety. Under daylight, the cell survival rates gradually decreased with the increase of Ce6 concentration, indicating that Ce6 had obvious phototoxicity under daylight. In addition, in the laser group, the cell survival rates of the Ce6 group, RCDs group, and RCDs-Ce6 group decreased gradually as the concentration increased, and the cell survival rates of the RCDs-Ce6 group were the lowest. IC50 values were calculated using SPSS Statistics software, and the results are shown in Table S2. The IC50 of Ce6 in the daylight group was lower, indicating that Ce6 had certain phototoxicity and poor biosafety, while the IC50 values of RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 were higher, the toxicity was negligible. Under 655 nm laser, the IC50 values of Ce6, RCDs, and RCDs-Ce6 groups significantly decreased. Among them, RCDs-Ce6 had the lowest IC50 value, indicating that it was the most substantial cell-killing effect. Formula 3 was used to calculate the combined index (CI) of PTT and PDT to judge the synergy of the photothermal and photodynamic effects. After calculation, CI= 0.278, which was a strong synergistic effect. NIH-3T3 cells were used to study the toxicity of drugs to normal cells. Compared with 4T1 cells, RCDs, Ce6 and RCDs-Ce6 showed lower toxicity (Fig. S18) and higher IC50 values (Table S3) to NIH-3T3 cells. The results showed that RCDs-Ce6 may have a better safety profile in normal cell lines, which may be related to the lower uptake of normal cells compared to cancer cells. These results indicated that RCDs-Ce6 had stronger lethality and lower phototoxicity.

To verify the therapeutic effect, 3D MCS was used to overcome the limitations of the 2D cell model. As shown in Fig. 5C, in the daylight group, it was found that RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 had less toxicity to 3D MCS, while Ce6 had more toxicity. In the laser group, it was found that the photothermal effect of RCDs and the photodynamic effect of Ce6 both produced killing effects on 3D MCS, and the combined treatment of RCDs-Ce6 (PTT and PDT) had the largest killing effect with the collapse of 3D MCS. These results suggest that RCDs-Ce6 had higher safety and efficacy than free Ce6 and that RCDs-Ce6 had good permeability to kill 3D MCS.

Co-staining experiments with green Calcein-AM and red PI further confirmed the phototoxicity and synergistic effects of RCDs-Ce6 on 4T1 cells (Fig. 5D). In the daylight group, a small amount of red fluorescence appeared in the free Ce6 group, while almost no red fluorescence in RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 group. This result demonstrates the low phototoxicity of RCDs and RCDs-Ce6. Under laser irradiation, compared with free Ce6 and RCDs, the RCDs-Ce6 group had the strongest red fluorescence and almost no green fluorescence, indicating the excellent synergistic effect of RCDs-Ce6. Furthermore, cell survival was judged by trypan blue staining, and the staining results are shown in Fig. S20. In the control group, a few cells were stained blue. Compared with PTT (RCDs) and PDT (free Ce6), almost all cells in the PTT and PDT group (RCDs-Ce6) were stained blue, indicating that the combined treatment group had the most potent killing ability to cells. To investigate the respective contribution of PTT and PDT to cytotoxicity in RCDs-Ce6, the PDT and PTT effects of RCDs-Ce6 were resisted separately by setting the conditions of adding Vc or constant temperature water bath [21]. As shown in Fig. S19, the cell viability in RCDs-Ce6 + 37 ℃ water bath group was lower than that in RCDs-Ce6 + Vc group. Furthermore, the cell viability in RCDs-Ce6 group was the lowest, showing an obviously combined treatment effect, which was similar to the results of cytotoxicity assay (Fig. 5B).

The Annexin V-FITC apoptosis kit was used to further detect apoptosis. As shown in Fig. 5E, the apoptosis and necrosis rate in the RCDs-Ce6 group was 71.6%, which was much higher than that in the RCDs (22.8%) and Ce6 (34.9%) group. The results were consistent with cytotoxic assays (Fig. 5B) and reflect the excellent synergistic killing effect of RCDs-Ce6.

Tumor-bearing Balb/c mice were injected with RCDs-Ce6 and Ce6 with the same Ce6 dose for fluorescence imaging within 0.5–24 h as shown in Fig. 6A. In the RCDs-Ce6 group, it was found that high fluorescence intensity at the tumor site at 0.5 h and continued to 24 h. At the same time, there was no evident fluorescence intensity in the tumor region of the Ce6 group. In ex vivo images of mice organs and tumors after 24 h post-injection (Fig. 6B). In the Ce6 group, the fluorescence intensities of the liver and lung were more substantial. Ce6 was trapped in the lung probably due to Ce6 easily aggregated into large particles in water. However, in the RCDs-Ce6 group, the fluorescence intensity of the tumor was the strongest, indicating that RCDs-Ce6 were more distributed in the tumor. The mean fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. 6C, at the tumor site, and the fluorescence intensity of RCDs-Ce6 was 2.70-fold higher than free Ce6. The high tumor-targeting ability of RCDs-Ce6 may be due to the EPR effect. These results suggested that compared with free Ce6, RCDs-Ce6 had a better in vivo imaging effect and more tumor distribution, implying that the nano-platform had excellent application potential in the integration of diagnosis and treatment.

In order to monitor and control the tumor temperature precisely during PTT, a thermal infrared imaging device was used. As shown in Fig. 7A and B, after administration with RCDs-Ce6, tumor temperature was detected at 0 h (control group), 2 h, 8 h, and 24 h. At 2 h, the temperature climbed highest within 6 min. Compared with the control group, tumor temperature in the RCDs-Ce6 group increased from 32.4 ℃ to 47.9 ℃ within 6 min, which was sufficient for photothermal therapy to kill the tumor. RCDs-Ce6 in vitro experiments demonstrated superior biocompatibility and synergistic therapeutic effects, and in vivo anti-tumor effects were further investigated using mammary adenocarcinoma (4T1) tumor-bearing mice. During the treatment period, mice's body weight was measured, and no significant weight loss was observed (Fig. 7C), indicating negligible toxicity in the treatment group. As displayed in Fig. 7D, tumors of mice in the normal saline group increased significantly within 15 days. At the same time, other treatment groups reduced tumors to varying degrees, but the RCDs-Ce6 + laser group (combination therapy) exhibited the most outstanding tumor inhibition efficiency. Similarly, tumor mass in each group after treatment and the typical photographs of tumors (Fig. 7E, F) additionally demonstrated the greater therapeutic effect of RCDs-Ce6 with laser (PDT and PTT).

Afterward, sections of major organs and tumors from the saline and treatment groups were stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). The synergistic therapy group showed significantly increased necrosis and apoptosis compared to other groups. (Fig. 7G). At the same time, the hemolytic experiments also showed the excellent safety of RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 (Table S4, Fig. S22). Additionally, in order to verify the systemic toxicity of RCDs-Ce6, the liver functions (aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT)) and kidney functions (blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine (CRE)) were evaluated. As shown in Fig. S23, RCDs-Ce6 displayed saline-comparable levels of biochemical indicators after three times treatment. In conclusion, based on the above outcomes, we can confirm that RCDs-Ce6 possesses enormous potential as an ideal laser-induced anti-tumor nano-platform for cancer therapy.

In this study, RCDs with good photothermal conversion ability were synthesized. To realize the co-excitation of PTT and PDT under the single laser and the integration of diagnosis and treatment, Ce6 was grafted with RCDs through amide reaction. RCDs enhanced the PDT effect of RCDs-Ce6 due to the FRET and well dispersion of Ce6. The RCDs in RCDs-Ce6 endowed the system with a good PTT effect and fluorescence imaging ability, simultaneously enhancing the system's retention ability in tumor tissue. Compared with free Ce6, RCDs-Ce6 can strengthen the effect with laser irradiation while reducing the phototoxicity owing to partial absorption of daylight by RCDs. In addition, RCDs-Ce6 exhibited a stronger ability to induce apoptosis and necrosis in tumor cells with lower toxicity to normal cells. Cytological and in vivo experiments showed that the synergistic tumor inhibitory effect of RCDs-Ce6 under 655 nm laser was better than that of a single treatment, with lower phototoxicity and excellent fluorescence imaging ability. In summary, this study further verified the drug delivery capability of RCDs with good photothermal conversion ability, low toxicity, abundant surface functional groups, and excellent biological imaging. At the same time, this provides a reference for the delivery of photosensitizers in tumor therapy.

Luming Song: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Review, Project administration. Hui Cheng: Validation, Review & Editing. Zhishuang Ren: Resources, Formal analysis. Huili Wang: Validation, Investigation. Junya Lu: Review & Editing. Jinghai Zhang: Resources, Validation. Qinfu Zhao: Conceptualization, Supervision, Review & Editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Siling Wang: Supervision, Review & Editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We have no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Grants from China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021M690483), Youth Project of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (No. LJKQZ2021030).
